content.text: Failure injection in staging tells you whether your code handles a fault. Failure injection in production tells you whether your entire system — including monitoring, on-call response, and user experience — handles it. Both are necessary, but production experiments carry real risk: a misconfigured experiment can amplify an existing latent fault into a user-facing outage.

Guardrails are the set of automated checks and organizational controls that prevent an experiment from running when conditions make it unsafe, and that stop it automatically if it exceeds its declared blast radius.

attrs.alt: Production chaos guardrail decision flow showing three gates before experiment runs

content.text: Gate 1: Never Run During an Active Incident

The first check is the hardest stop: if any P0 or P1 alert is currently firing, the experiment must not start. Running chaos during an active incident adds noise to an already degraded system, complicates root cause analysis, and risks compounding the failure.
Automate this gate — do not rely on the engineer running the experiment to manually check PagerDuty. Human checks are skipped under time pressure; automated gates are not.

Many Claude integration failures amplify under load. Inject failures only during a pre-approved traffic window where impact stays bounded. Define the window as a combination of time-of-day and observed request rate.
Avoid peak hours; reduce user exposure
Cron gate: allowed only 01:00–05:00 local time
Prometheus check: rps < N before starting
Scope experiment to a fraction of workers/pods
Feature flag or Kubernetes label selector
Ensure capacity exists to absorb extra load

import os
from datetime import datetime, time
import requests


PROMETHEUS_URL = os.environ.get("PROMETHEUS_URL", "http://localhost:9090")
ALLOWED_START = time(1, 0)   # 01:00
ALLOWED_END = time(5, 0)     # 05:00
MAX_RPS = 50                  # requests per second across all workers


def check_traffic_window() -> None:
    now = datetime.now().time()
    if not (ALLOWED_START <= now <= ALLOWED_END):
        raise RuntimeError(
            f"CHAOS ABORTED: current time {now} is outside allowed window "
            f"({ALLOWED_START}–{ALLOWED_END})"
        )


def check_rps() -> None:
    resp = requests.get(
        f"{PROMETHEUS_URL}/api/v1/query",
        params={"query": 'sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[1m]))'},
        timeout=10,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    results = resp.json()["data"]["result"]
    current_rps = float(results[0]["value"][1]) if results else 0.0
    if current_rps > MAX_RPS:
        raise RuntimeError(
            f"CHAOS ABORTED: current RPS {current_rps:.1f} exceeds max {MAX_RPS}"
        )

If the system is already degraded when the experiment starts, any observed failures during the experiment are uninterpretable — you cannot tell whether chaos or an existing bug caused them. Run a baseline health check as the final pre-flight gate.
Elevated baseline means system is already stressed
Full queue means experiment will immediately drop jobs
Open circuit means the fault already fired
Replication lag indicates infra stress outside Claude

Once an experiment is running, a stop-condition monitor continuously evaluates whether the experiment has exceeded its declared blast radius. The moment a threshold is breached, the monitor triggers an automatic rollback.

import asyncio
import signal
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class StopCondition:
    max_error_rate: float = 0.02      # 2% error rate on non-canary traffic
    max_p99_seconds: float = 10.0     # 10 s p99
    max_queue_pct: float = 0.90       # 90% queue fill
    check_interval_seconds: float = 15.0


async def stop_condition_monitor(
    condition: StopCondition,
    rollback_fn: "Callable[[], Awaitable[None]]",
) -> None:
    """Run until stop condition fires or cancelled."""
    while True:
        await asyncio.sleep(condition.check_interval_seconds)

        error_rate = query_prometheus(
            'sum(rate(claude_api_errors_total{env!="canary"}[1m])) '
            '/ sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total{env!="canary"}[1m]))'
        )
        if error_rate > condition.max_error_rate:
            print(f"STOP CONDITION: non-canary error rate {error_rate:.3%} > {condition.max_error_rate:.3%}")
            await rollback_fn()
            return

        p99 = query_prometheus(
            'histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(claude_api_duration_seconds_bucket{env!="canary"}[1m]))'
        )
        if p99 > condition.max_p99_seconds:
            print(f"STOP CONDITION: p99 {p99:.2f}s > {condition.max_p99_seconds:.2f}s")
            await rollback_fn()
            return


async def rollback() -> None:
    """Disable chaos feature flag and alert on-call."""
    # 1. Flip feature flag
    # 2. Scale down canary deployment
    # 3. Page on-call
    print("Rollback complete")


@asynccontextmanager
async def chaos_experiment(condition: StopCondition):
    monitor_task = asyncio.create_task(
        stop_condition_monitor(condition, rollback)
    )
    try:
        yield
    finally:
        monitor_task.cancel()
        try:
            await monitor_task
        except asyncio.CancelledError:
            pass
Set the stop condition threshold tighter than your SLO. If your error-rate SLO is 1%, set the stop condition at 0.5% so the experiment halts before you breach the SLO, not after.

Canary scoping restricts the experiment to a small fraction of production traffic. The cleanest implementation uses a Kubernetes label selector to route a fixed percentage of workers to the chaos-injected pool.

# chaos-worker-canary.yaml — 5% of workers run with chaos injection
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: claude-worker-chaos-canary
  labels:
    app: claude-worker
    chaos: enabled
spec:
  replicas: 1              # 1 canary pod vs. 19 normal pods = 5%
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: claude-worker
      chaos: enabled
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: claude-worker
        chaos: enabled
        env: canary
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: worker
          image: your-registry/claude-worker:latest
          env:
            - name: CHAOS_PROFILE
              value: "timeout"         # set to off on normal deployments
            - name: CHAOS_TIMEOUT_RATE
              value: "0.10"            # 10% of calls get 35s timeout
---
# Normal deployment (unchanged)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: claude-worker
spec:
  replicas: 19
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: claude-worker
        chaos: disabled
        env: production

With this setup, Prometheus can filter `env!="canary"` to measure whether the experiment leaks into the non-chaos pod pool. If error rate rises in non-canary pods, the blast radius has escaped the canary scope.
Every experiment must have a documented rollback that completes in under 60 seconds. The rollback procedure must be tested in staging before the production experiment runs.
Toggle flag to false in flag service (e.g. LaunchDarkly)

#!/bin/bash
# chaos-rollback.sh — emergency rollback for any active chaos experiment
set -euo pipefail

echo "[rollback] Disabling chaos environment variable..."
kubectl set env deployment/claude-worker-chaos-canary CHAOS_PROFILE=off

echo "[rollback] Scaling canary to zero..."
kubectl scale deployment/claude-worker-chaos-canary --replicas=0

echo "[rollback] Waiting for canary pods to terminate..."
kubectl rollout status deployment/claude-worker-chaos-canary --timeout=60s

echo "[rollback] Verifying error rate returning to baseline..."
sleep 30
python3 - <<'EOF'
import requests, os
url = os.environ.get("PROMETHEUS_URL", "http://localhost:9090")
r = requests.get(f"{url}/api/v1/query",
    params={"query": 'sum(rate(claude_api_errors_total[1m])) / sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[1m]))'})
rate = float(r.json()["data"]["result"][0]["value"][1])
print(f"Current error rate: {rate:.4%}")
assert rate < 0.005, f"Error rate {rate:.4%} still elevated after rollback"
EOF

echo "[rollback] Complete."
Not all guardrails are equally hard. Distinguish automated hard stops from advisory warnings, and document which can be overridden by whom.
Send Slack message to #oncall before start

Hard stops are enforced by the pre-flight gate script — they cannot be skipped by the operator. Advisory warnings appear in the experiment log but do not halt execution. Never demote a hard stop to advisory without a post-mortem review of the rationale.

Every production chaos experiment must produce an audit log entry that records who ran it, when, what was injected, what the stop-condition thresholds were, and what the measured outcomes were. This log is required for post-run review and error budget accounting.

import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from datetime import datetime, timezone


@dataclass
class ExperimentRecord:
    experiment_id: str
    operator: str                    # e.g. "alice@example.com"
    started_at: str                  # ISO 8601
    ended_at: str
    end_reason: str                  # "completed", "stop_condition", "manual_abort"
    chaos_profile: str               # e.g. "timeout:0.10"
    canary_replicas: int
    baseline_error_rate_pct: float
    peak_error_rate_pct: float       # measured during experiment
    peak_p99_seconds: float
    stop_condition_triggered: bool
    error_budget_consumed_minutes: float


def write_experiment_record(record: ExperimentRecord, log_path: str) -> None:
    with open(log_path, "a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(asdict(record)) + "\n")
    print(f"Experiment record written: {record.experiment_id}")

# Example:
# write_experiment_record(
#     ExperimentRecord(
#         experiment_id="chaos-2026-07-19-timeout-01",
#         operator="alice@example.com",
#         started_at="2026-07-19T02:00:00Z",
#         ended_at="2026-07-19T02:30:00Z",
#         end_reason="completed",
#         chaos_profile="timeout:0.10",
#         canary_replicas=1,
#         baseline_error_rate_pct=0.02,
#         peak_error_rate_pct=1.20,
#         peak_p99_seconds=36.4,
#         stop_condition_triggered=False,
#         error_budget_consumed_minutes=3.2,
#     ),
#     log_path="chaos-experiments.ndjson",
# )

Production failure injection requires three automated pre-flight gates — no active incidents, correct traffic window, healthy baseline — plus a continuous stop-condition monitor and a rollback that completes in under 60 seconds.
Gate 1: query PagerDuty for active P0/P1 alerts; abort if any are triggered or acknowledged.
Gate 2: verify the current time is within the approved window and that request rate is below the experiment maximum.
Gate 3: check baseline error rate (< 0.5%), p99 latency (< 2× SLO), and queue depth (< 80% capacity).
Scope experiments to a canary deployment using Kubernetes labels; monitor non-canary traffic with env!="canary" Prometheus selectors.
Run a stop-condition monitor every 15 seconds; trigger rollback automatically if non-canary error rate or p99 exceeds the declared threshold.
Log every experiment to an audit trail including operator, duration, peak signals, and error budget consumed.
